scene.name: The Lamplit Green, Where the Little Ones Are Kept Warm

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Brigid
npcs.title: who keeps the lamp lit for the children who came too soon
npcs.hail: *(A broad, warm, work-worn woman sits on a log by a small fire, and around her — playing, squabbling, climbing the two pale trees, drawing in the dust, doing all the loud bright ordinary things — are children. A great many children. And you understand, with a lurch, where you are, and what these are: the little ones. The ones who came through death too soon — the fevers, the hard winters, the thousand small cruelties that take the young — souls that crossed over with their whole lives still folded up unlived inside them. The woman tending them looks up at you with the unhurried, unbreakable steadiness of someone who long ago decided that the most important work in all the afterlife was being done right here, on this lamplit green, and that she would do it.)* …Mind the chalk drawings — they're proud of those. *(a warm, low voice)* …You're cold. Crossed-over. Returned, even. *(she nods slowly)* …I'm Brigid. I keep the green. Somebody has to, you see — the little ones come through frightened and small and *furious*, most of them, and the great powers of this City don't quite know what to *do* with a soul that has no deeds to weigh and no debts to settle and nothing to its name but a half-drawn picture and a list of all the things it was *going* to do. *(her jaw sets, gently)* …So I sit with them. And I keep the lamp lit. And I will tell you the thing I have learned, tending the youngest dead for longer than I'll say — and you should hear it, before you walk on to whatever grey Wall you're walking toward: …there is no soul in all the worlds, cold one, that the Wall's whole *ledger* makes a *bigger* fool of …than a child who never got to *do* a single thing …and was *worth* the *entire* *sky* *anyway.**

npcs.name: Nettle
npcs.title: who is angry, and is right to be
npcs.hail: *(A girl of perhaps nine sits apart from the others with her arms crossed and her chin set, glaring at the world with the pure, undiluted, righteous fury that only a child can manage — a fury with no self-pity in it at all, just a blazing, total certainty that a wrong has been done. She has a stick, and she has been using it to gouge angry lines in the dust, and as you approach she points it at you like a sword, daring you.)* …Don't. *(fierce)* …Don't you DARE do the thing. The grown-up thing, where you go all soft and sad and you say *'oh, sweetheart'* in the *voice*, and you tell me it's all right and I'm in a better place and everything happens for a reason and I should be at PEACE. *(her eyes are hot)* …It is NOT all right. This is NOT a better place — better than WHAT, I wasn't done with the FIRST one! And there's no REASON, there's no reason at all, I was NINE, and I was going to — *(her voice cracks, but the anger holds it together)* …I had a LIST. I had a whole list of things I was going to do, and now I can't do ANY of them, not ONE, forever, and everybody keeps wanting me to be CALM about it. *(she grips the stick)* …So if you're going to do the voice, cold one, you can just go away. …But if you're not — *(a flicker, wary, testing)* …if you're going to let me be as angry as I actually AM …then maybe you can stay. …Nobody lets me be as angry as I actually am. …And I am SO. ANGRY.*

npcs.name: Lyssa
npcs.title: who is not scared anymore, but her mother is
npcs.hail: *(A small girl sits cross-legged in the dust with a half-finished chalk drawing in front of her, and she is not crying, and she is not afraid — she has the calm, careful, slightly-too-grown-up gentleness of a child who has done her own grieving and come out the other side, and is now worried about somebody else. She looks up at you with serious eyes.)* …Are you going back? *(quietly, before any greeting)* …Brigid says some souls go back. The Returned ones. She says you're one, maybe. *(she sets down her chalk and folds her small hands, very serious, the way a child is serious when something matters more than anything)* …Because if you're going back — to where the living are — then I need to ask you something, and it's the most important thing, and I've been waiting and waiting for someone who could do it. *(her careful calm wobbles, just slightly)* …It's my mother. …I'm not scared anymore, you see — I WAS, at first, it's dark when you come through and you don't know where anybody is — but Brigid found me, and it's warm here, and I'm all right now, I really am. *(her chin lifts)* …But my mother doesn't KNOW that. …She thinks I'm still scared. …She cried so much, cold one. She cried like — and she still does, I can feel it sometimes, the way you can feel rain coming. …And she can't hear me, however loud I — *(she steadies herself, fierce and gentle)* …Can you carry her a message? …Please? …Just so she knows. …She's so sad, and I'm all right, and she doesn't KNOW, and somebody has to TELL her.*

npcs.name: Pim
npcs.title: the littlest, who has been here longest
npcs.hail: *(Sitting very small at the edge of the firelight is the littlest one — five, perhaps, or the size of five, though something in the patient, faded quality of the small soul suggests it has been five for a very, very long time — and it is holding a worn scrap of something, a blanket or a toy worn down past recognizing, and watching the other children play with the wistful patience of one who has watched a great many children come, and grow bright, and be remembered, and move on, while it stayed.)* …Hello. *(a small, soft voice)* …I'm Pim. I'm the littlest. I've been here the longest, too, which is funny, that the littlest is the longest, but Brigid says it's because — *(a pause, working it out)* …because the ones who love you keep you bright, and when they stop, you go soft and faded, like my blanket. *(it holds up the worn scrap)* …And I've been here SO long, cold one, that I think — *(the small voice doesn't waver, but something behind it does)* …I think the ones who loved me must have stopped. Not because they were mean. Brigid says nobody means to. It's just been so long that everybody who knew my face is here now too, or gone on, and there's nobody left down in the living place who remembers there was ever a Pim at all. *(it looks up at you, and the question is the smallest and largest thing you have ever been asked)* …Do you — when you go back — do you think you could find out? …If anybody still remembers me? …Or am I all the way faded now? …It's all right if I am. …I just want to KNOW.*

variants.text: *(She shifts to make room on the log, and a small child immediately climbs into the space, and she settles it against her side without breaking her gaze from you, the motion of a soul that has done it ten thousand times.)* …Sit, if the little ones will let you — they'll want to know your name and whether you can do any tricks, fair warning. *(a warm, tired smile)* …I was a midwife, cold one. In life. I caught the young coming *into* the world for forty years — the slippery, screaming, furious *arrival* of them — and I lost some, the way every midwife does, the ones that came too soon or too still or too sick, and I grieved every one like my own. *(she strokes the hair of the child against her side)* …So when I died, and crossed, and found *this* — a whole green full of the ones who came too soon, frightened and small and with no one to tend them — *(she shrugs, a vast and gentle thing)* …well. It was the same work, cold one. Only now I catch them coming *out.* …Ask me what you like. …But know that I am *fierce* about these little ones, cold one — *fiercer* than I am about anything — because they are the ones your grey Wall understands *least*, and wrongs *worst.* …And if you've come to learn the thing that brings that Wall *down* …you could not have come to a *better* green to learn it.*

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(She keeps one arm around the dozing child and gives you the rest of her attention, warm and unhurried, with the deep groundedness of a soul entirely certain of the worth of its work.)* …Go on and ask, cold one. *(a low smile)* …I've tended the youngest dead longer than I tended the living, now, and there's little about them I haven't had time to understand. …And it's good to be *asked.* The little ones don't ask; they just *climb* on you. Being *asked* is a grown-up kindness I don't get much of, down here among the small.

choices.text: *(read the keeper of the green — past the warmth, to what it is to tend the souls no one knows how to weigh)* "Let me look at you a moment, Brigid. At what it takes to keep this lamp lit."
"Tell me about them, Brigid — the little ones. What does a soul that came too soon actually need, that the great powers can't give it?"

*(the one who knows a child with no deeds is the Wall's nakedest fraud — worth was never what you did)* "Brigid — you said it first, so let me say it back, because it's the thing that breaks the whole Wall. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs every soul by its *deeds.* What did you do, what did you build, what did you earn, what are you owed. And then it shows you a child. A soul that died at nine, at five, at five *days* — that never did one single deed, never earned one single thing, never built or owed or accomplished anything at all, that has nothing to its name but a half-drawn picture and a list of things it was *going* to do. And the Wall's whole ledger comes up *blank.* By the Wall's own measure, a child is worth *nothing* — no deeds, no debts, an empty page. And every soul knows, *knows* in its bones, that this is *obscene* — that a child is worth the entire *sky*, worth *everything*, precious past all weighing — and a child *never did a thing to earn it.* Which means worth was *never* about deeds, Brigid. It never could have been. If the most worthless soul on the Wall's ledger is the one every heart knows is most precious, then the ledger is measuring the *wrong thing* — measuring *doing* when worth was always about *being*, about being *here*, being *alive* once, being *loved.* The children don't *fail* the Wall's test, Brigid. They *expose* it. They're the proof, written in the smallest hand there is, that the whole ledger was a lie."
"There are three I see, set a little apart — the angry one, the gentle one with the chalk, the littlest by the fire. Tell me about them, Brigid, before I go to them."
"Keep the lamp lit, Brigid. I'll come back — and I'll have spoken with your little ones."

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, easy and unguarded, rocking the dozing child a little, and you let your cold move past the warmth to the substance of the soul that keeps this green.)* *(And your sense finds, beneath the unhurried steadiness, a grief so vast and so well-carried that you mistake it at first for peace. Your sense reads the forty years of midwifery, and reads the ones she lost — each one held, each one named, each one grieved as her own — and reads that she did not, as most would, let those losses harden her or break her; she let them *teach* her, until she understood the young the way few souls ever do: that a life's worth is not its *length*, that a soul who lived nine years was not nine-tenths cheated of some fuller life it was owed, that each child was *whole*, complete, a finished and total person in the years it had, however few. And your sense reads what she does on this green, and it is not what it appears. She appears to comfort. But your sense reads deeper: she does not lie to the little ones, does not tell the angry ones to be calm or the grieving ones that everything is fine. She *witnesses* them — lets the furious be furious, lets the sad be sad, lets each small soul be exactly as big as its feeling, because she learned, catching the young for forty years, that a child can survive almost anything except being told its feelings are too large for it. And your sense reads the fierceness in her, the thing she warned you of, and finds it is the fierceness of a soul that has watched the great cold powers of the afterlife look at a child and see an *empty ledger*, a soul with nothing to weigh, a *problem* — and has set herself, immovably, between those powers and the little ones, a midwife-shaped wall of her own, saying: *you will not measure them by what they did. They are children. They were never here to do. They were here to be, and to be loved, and that was always enough, and I will keep this lamp lit against your whole grey arithmetic until the worlds end.* Your sense reads that Brigid is not tending the children out of pity. She is defending them. And she has never once lost.)*

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, settling the sleeping child, and when she speaks it is with the plain authority of someone who has learned a true thing the hard way.)* …What they need. *(a low breath)* …You'd think it was *comfort*, cold one. Softness. To be told it's all right. That's what the *grown* powers try, the few that try at all — they come to the green with their *gentle* *voices* and their *'there, there, little one, you're at peace now'* — and the children *hate* it, cold one. *Hate* it. *(she almost smiles)* …Because it's a *lie*, and children *know* lies, they've not yet learned to be *polite* about them. A child who died at nine is *not* at peace, and telling her she is only teaches her that her *real* feeling — the *fury*, the *grief*, the *robbed* feeling — is *wrong*, is *too much*, is a thing she must *hide* to be *acceptable.* *(her jaw sets)* …What they *need*, cold one — the thing I learned, and the thing the gentle-voiced powers never will — is to be *believed.* …The angry one needs someone to say *'you're RIGHT, it's NOT fair, be as angry as you want, I'll sit here while you are.'* The grieving one needs someone to take her grief *seriously*, not *soothe* it away. The frightened one needs *not* to be told there's nothing to fear, but to be *held* while she fears it. *(she meets your eyes)* …A child is a *whole* *person*, cold one, with *whole* feelings — they are *small* and they are *new* but they are *not* *less* — and the *worst* thing you can do to a small new whole person is *decide* its feelings are too big for it and try to make them *smaller.* *(quietly)* …They don't need *comfort*, cold one. …They need to be *taken* *seriously.* …It is the *one* dignity …the world *never* *grants* the *young.* …And it is the *only* thing …any of them …has ever *actually* *needed* …from *me.**

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(Her gaze moves across the green to the three you mean, and her face softens with a particular love for each.)* …Ah. You've a good eye, cold one. Those three. *(she nods toward the girl with the stick)* …*Nettle.* Nine years old and *furious* — and *right* to be, mind, don't you *dare* think otherwise. She's got a *list*, cold one, all the things she meant to do, and she *rages* at being robbed of them, and *every* grown soul that's come to this green has tried to *calm* her, and *every* one has *failed*, because calming her is the *one* wrong thing. *Don't* calm her, cold one. *Be angry* *with* her. It's all she's ever wanted. *(her gaze shifts to the small girl with the chalk)* …*Lyssa.* She's done her *own* grieving, that one — brave as anything — and now she frets only for her *mother*, who's still *down* there, *crying*, not *knowing* her girl's all right. *(quietly)* …If you're *truly* going back, cold one — *Lyssa* has a thing to ask you, and you should *say* *yes*, and you should *mean* it, because a child's message *carried* *faithfully* is no small magic. *(and her gaze comes to rest, gentlest of all, on the littlest by the fire)* …And *Pim.* *(her voice goes very soft)* …The littlest. Here the *longest.* Pim is — *(she pauses)* …Pim is *fading*, cold one. The young stay bright as long as the living *remember* them, and Pim's living are all *gone* now, *grieved* out or *passed* on, and there's no one *left* down there who *holds* the memory of a Pim. …And a child no one remembers …goes *soft*, and *faint*, and at last — *(she stops)* …*Go* to Pim *last*, cold one. …And *gently.* …Pim has the *hardest* question of the three. …And I have *never* …found a *good* answer to it. …Maybe a *Returned* soul …can.*

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(She listens, and her arm tightens around the sleeping child, and slow tears stand in her steady eyes, and when you finish she nods — a deep, fierce, final nod, the nod of a defender hearing the case she has argued alone for an age finally spoken back to her in full.)* …*'They don't fail the test. They expose it.'* *(her voice is thick)* …Cold one. …I have *known* it — *here* — *(she presses a hand to her chest)* …for as long as I've kept this green. That there was something *obscene* in the way the great powers *looked* at my little ones, something *backwards*, but I'm a *midwife*, not a *philosopher*, I never had the *words* for *why* it was *backwards.* *(fiercely, through tears)* …And you've *given* them. …*'If the most worthless soul on the ledger is the one every heart knows is most precious, then the ledger measures the wrong thing.'* …*Yes.* …*That's* it. …*That's* the thing I've stood on this green *defending* without being able to *name.* …A child has *no* *deeds* — and is *worth* the *sky* — and *both* of those are *true at once*, cold one, which means *deeds were never where the worth was.* *(she rises, settling the child gently onto the log, and stands to her full broad height, and she is magnificent)* …And *now* I have the *words*, cold one, and *you* must *carry* them to that *Wall.* *(her voice rings across the green)* …*Tell* it: …you have been *weighing* *doing*, when worth was *always* *being.* …And the *proof* is on *my* *green* — a *hundred* *small* *souls* who *never* *did* a *single* *thing* …and are worth *more* than every *deed* in your whole *grey* *ledger* *combined.* *(she meets your eyes, fierce and weeping and triumphant)* …*Go* and *break* it, cold one. …Not with an *army.* …With a *child's* *empty* *page* …that *every* *heart* *in* *all* the *worlds* …already *knows* …is *priceless.* …*That* is the thing the Wall *cannot* *survive*: …a *soul* it *must* call *worthless* …that *no* one, *anywhere*, has *ever* *believed* was *anything* …*less* than *everything.**

nodes.speaker: Brigid
nodes.text: *(She gathers the sleeping child back against her side and gives you a warm, fierce nod.)* …Go on, cold one. Go and meet them. *(a low smile)* …And remember what I told you: *don't* do the gentle-voice. *Don't* tell them it's all right, or they're at peace, or it happened for a reason. They'll *see* the lie and they'll *close* to you, and they'll be *right* to. *(quietly)* …*Believe* them. The angry one's *fury*, the gentle one's *grief*, the littlest one's *fear.* *Take* it *seriously.* *Sit* in it *with* them. …That's the whole of the craft, cold one — the thing forty years of catching the young taught me — *(she meets your eyes)* …you don't *fix* a child's feeling. …You don't *shrink* it. …You *honor* it, and you *stay*, and you let the small new whole person be *exactly* as big as it *actually* *feels* — …and *that*, cold one, *that* is the thing that *no* one *ever* *does* for the *young* …and the *only* thing any of them …has *ever* *wanted.* …*Go.* …And *come* *back* and *tell* me how they *were.* …I *love* …to *hear* …that someone *finally* *listened.**

conversations.title: Nettle, Who Is Right To Be Angry

variants.text: *(She keeps the stick pointed at you, eyes narrowed, every inch of her braced for the gentle-voice betrayal.)* …Okay. You're still here. *(suspicious)* …But you haven't SAID anything yet, so I don't know if you're a do-the-voice one or not. *(she lowers the stick a fraction)* …Here's the test, cold one. I'm going to tell you a TRUE thing, and you're going to do one of two things. *(she takes a breath, and it comes out white-hot)* …I was NINE. And I got a fever, and it was stupid, it wasn't even a GOOD way, it wasn't brave or important, I just got HOT and then I got cold and then I was HERE, and I didn't get to — *(her voice rises, furious)* …I didn't get to grow UP. I didn't get to be TEN. I had a whole LIST, cold one, I was going to learn the long songs and ride a horse by myself and see the ocean and I was going to be TALLER than my brother and tell him so, and I was going to — *(she's shaking with it)* …and now I CAN'T. None of it. EVER. *(she glares at you, daring)* …So. There it is. …Now you either say *'oh sweetheart, it's all right'* — and then you can go AWAY — …or you say the OTHER thing. …The TRUE thing. …Go on, cold one. …Which one are you?*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(She sits back down, but keeps the stick across her knees, still half-braced, watching you with the fierce wariness of a child who has been disappointed by adults too many times to trust one easily.)* …Fine. Ask me stuff. *(a sharp look)* …But I'm WATCHING you, cold one. The second you do the voice — the SECOND — I'm done. I mean it. I've got nothing but time to be done with people in. *(a beat, then, grudging)* …But you can ask. …Most people don't ask the angry kid anything. …They just want me to STOP.

choices.text: *(read the angry child — past the fury, to whether the fury is the wound or the armor over it)* "Hold still a second, Nettle. Let me really look at you — at all that anger, and what's underneath it."
"Tell me the list, Nettle. All of it. Every single thing you were going to do. I want to hear the whole list."

*(don't soothe her — be angry WITH her; meet the fury with agreement, not calm)* "You want the true thing, Nettle? Here it is. You're RIGHT. It is NOT all right. It is NOT fair, and there is NO reason, and anyone who tells you to be at peace about it is LYING to you. You were nine. You had a list. And it was STOLEN — a stupid pointless fever stole every single thing on it, forever, and that is an OUTRAGE, and you should be angry, you should be FURIOUS, you have every right to the biggest anger there has ever been. I'm not going to calm you down, Nettle. I'm going to be angry WITH you. Because you're not wrong to feel this. You're the only one feeling the right-sized feeling about a wrong this big."

*(the one who knows the Wall would tell a robbed child to settle her account — and that her fury is a truer measure of her worth than any ledger)* "Nettle — the grey Wall I'm walking toward would do to you the worst version of the voice. It would look at you — nine years old, a fever, a stolen list — and it would tell you to *settle down*, to *accept your place*, to stop *making a fuss* about a thing that's simply *how it is.* It would call your fury a *disorder*, a thing to be *managed*, because the Wall *needs* souls to be calm, to accept, to stop demanding what they're owed. And your fury, Nettle — your refusal to *settle*, your blazing insistence that being robbed of your whole life is an OUTRAGE — that's not a thing to be fixed. It's the truest thing on this whole green. You are *measuring the wrong correctly.* A soul that shrugs at its own stolen life has been taught not to know its own worth. You *know* yours — you know it was worth a whole life, a list, an ocean you'll never see — and your anger is just *that knowledge*, refusing to be quiet. Keep it, Nettle. Don't ever let them calm it out of you. The day a robbed soul stops being angry is the day the Wall has won."
"I'll come back, Nettle — and I still won't do the voice. Promise."

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(She glares, but she holds still, daring you to find something soft to pity, and you let your cold move past the blazing fury to what is under it — and what you find is not, as you half-expected, a frightened child hiding behind anger.)* *(Your sense finds that the anger is not armor. That is the thing that stops you. With most souls the rage is a wall thrown up over grief or fear, and the work is to reach past it — but your sense reads, in Nettle, that the anger *is* the true feeling, clean and entire, not covering anything, not defending anything, simply the correct and proportionate response of a whole soul to an enormous wrong. Your sense reads that she is not, underneath, secretly sad and using anger to hide it; she is *both*, fully, the grief and the fury side by side, and the fury is not the lesser or the falser of the two. And your sense reads *why* the anger matters so much to her, why she fights so hard to be allowed it: because every adult who has ever tried to *calm* her has been, without knowing it, trying to make her *agree* that what happened to her was *acceptable* — and it was *not* acceptable, and Nettle knows it was not, and her anger is the only thing she has left that still *insists* on the truth. To give up the anger would be to *concede* — to say *yes, all right, being robbed of my whole life at nine is a thing I can be at peace with* — and Nettle is far too honest, and far too alive, even in death, to concede a lie that large. Your sense reads that her fury is not a sickness or a stage or a thing to be worked through. It is her *integrity.* It is a nine-year-old soul refusing, with everything it has, to pretend that a monstrous thing is fine. And your sense reads, last, the thing that would break the gentle-voiced powers if they could see it: that what Nettle needs is not to have the anger *removed*, but to have it *joined* — to have, just once, one single soul stand beside her and say *you are right, I am angry too* — because the loneliest thing in all the worlds is to be the only one furious about a wrong that everyone else has agreed to call peace.)*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(She blinks — clearly no one has ever ASKED for the list before, only tried to make her stop reciting it — and something in her fierce face shifts, off-balance, and then she sits up straight and tells you, fast and fierce and precise, because the list is sacred.)* …Okay. The list. *(she counts on her fingers, hard)* …I was going to learn the long songs — the real ones, the ones that take all winter, not the baby ones. I was going to ride a horse BY MYSELF, no leading rein, and Da said maybe at ten and I was SO CLOSE. I was going to see the ocean, because Gran saw it once and said it was bigger than thinking and I didn't believe her and I wanted to find out. *(faster now, fierce)* …I was going to get taller than my brother Col and tell him to his FACE. I was going to have a dog. I was going to learn which mushrooms were the poison ones so I'd stop being scared of the woods. I was going to kiss somebody, eventually, probably, to see what the fuss was. I was going to grow UP, cold one, and find out what I'd BE — *(her voice catches, but she pushes through, because the list must be finished)* …and that's the worst one. That's the one at the bottom. …I didn't get to find out *what I'd be.* …Everybody else gets to find out what they'd be. …I just — *stopped.* At nine. Half-made. …*(she looks at you, fierce and wet)* …That's the list, cold one. …Nobody ever lets me get to the BOTTOM of it. …They always stop me before *what I'd be.* …Because that's the one …that makes the grown-ups …have to feel it.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(You don't soften. You don't soothe. You meet her blazing fury with your own — agree, completely, that it is an outrage, that she was robbed, that her anger is the right size for the wrong — and you do it without one drop of the gentle-voice, without trying to lead her anywhere calmer, just standing in the fire beside her. And the girl who has been bracing for betrayal since you walked up looks at you with utter astonishment, and then her face crumples, not into calm, but into the fiercest relief you have ever seen.)* …you — *(she can barely speak)* …you said it BACK. you said it's NOT fair and there's NO reason and I'm RIGHT to be angry, and you didn't — *(she scrubs furiously at her eyes)* …you didn't try to make it SMALLER. *(and then it pours out, an age of it)* …Do you know what it's LIKE, cold one? Everybody — EVERYBODY — wants me to stop. To be calm. To be a *good* dead girl who *accepts* it. And every time they try, it's like they're saying the thing that happened was *okay*, that I should be FINE with it, and I am NOT fine with it, I will NEVER be fine with it, and being the only one who's still ANGRY is so — *(her voice breaks)* …it's so LONELY, cold one. Being the only one who thinks it's still WRONG. *(she grips your sleeve, fierce)* …And you just — you stood RIGHT in it with me. You didn't pull me OUT, you got IN. *(a wild, wet, furious laugh)* …I'm still angry. I'm going to STAY angry, it's the only HONEST thing. …But it's not LONELY anymore. *(she wipes her face, fierce and clear)* …That's the thing nobody understood, cold one. …I never needed to stop being angry. …I just needed somebody to be angry WITH me, so I wasn't the only one left telling the truth. …You did that. …You're the first one who ever did that. …Don't you DARE start doing the voice now. …Just — stay angry with me a minute. …It's the nicest thing anybody's ever done.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(You agree with her — it's not fair, she's right to be angry — and you mostly keep the gentle-voice out of it, and she watches you closely, testing every word, and slowly, grudgingly, the stick lowers all the way.)* …Okay. *(wary, but thawing)* …Okay, you didn't do the voice. Mostly. There was a LITTLE bit of voice, right at the end, but mostly you — you actually SAID it's not fair. Out loud. Like you meant it. *(she studies you)* …Most grown souls CAN'T say that, you know. They get this look, like the words won't come out, like agreeing that it's NOT fair would — I don't know — break something in them. So they do the voice instead. *(she pulls her knees up)* …You said it, though. Mostly. *(a grudging, real thing)* …Come back, cold one. And next time — *(fierce)* …next time say it like you're NOT scared of it. The whole thing. That it's wrong and there's no reason and it should never have happened. …All the way. …No little bit of voice at the end. *(quietly)* …I want to hear ONE grown-up say the whole true thing all the way through …without flinching at the end. …I think it might be the thing I've been waiting for. …Come back and say it ALL the way.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(You try to agree with her — but the habit is too strong, and it curdles, halfway through, into reassurance: it's not fair, BUT you're safe now, BUT you're not alone, BUT it'll be okay. And her eyes narrow, and the stick comes back up.)* …There it is. *(flat)* …You ALMOST didn't do it. You got all the way to *'it's not fair'* and I thought — for a second I thought you were a real one. And then you said *but.* *(fierce, disappointed)* …There's always a *but*, cold one. *'It's not fair, BUT.'* And everything after the *but* is just the voice wearing a disguise. *'But you're at peace. But it's better here. But you're not alone.'* *(she jabs the stick at you)* …The *but* is how you take it BACK. The *but* is how you say *'okay yes it's wrong but please stop being angry about it because your anger makes ME uncomfortable.'* *(quietly, hard)* …I don't want a *but*, cold one. …I want somebody to say *'it's not fair'* and then just — STOP. Let it be not-fair. Don't rescue it. Don't fix it. Don't make me feel better about it. …Just let it be the wrong that it IS, and be angry next to me, and DON'T add the part that makes it smaller. *(she turns away)* …Come back when you can leave off the *but*. …That's the whole test, cold one. …Everybody fails it. …But maybe you won't, next time. …You got closer than most.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(It goes exactly wrong — you reach, by deep adult reflex, for the worst comfort of all: that she's in a better place now, that her suffering is over, that there's a plan, that it happened for a reason. And the stick drops, and her whole face shuts like a door, and when she speaks it is quiet and cold and far worse than her fury.)* …Oh. *(flat)* …You're one of THEM. *(she turns her face away)* …*A better place.* *A reason.* …You know what that does, cold one, when you say that? *A reason?* *(her voice shakes, but it's gone cold now, not hot)* …It means the fever that killed me at nine was *supposed* to. That somebody *wanted* it. That my whole stolen list was part of some *plan* and I should be *grateful*, or at least *quiet*, because it all *means* something. *(barely)* …It doesn't mean anything. It was a STUPID fever and it took everything and there was NO reason, and the second you say there was, you're telling me I should make PEACE with the thing that ROBBED me, and you're telling me my anger — the only true thing I've got left — is just me being too DUMB to see the *plan.* *(she draws her knees up tight, small and shut)* …Go away. …You did the worst one. …Not *'it's all right'* — the OTHER worst one. *'It's all right BECAUSE it means something.'* …Go away, and don't come back unless you can un-know that. …There was no reason, cold one. …I was nine, and it was wrong, and it meant NOTHING. …And until you can sit with THAT — with the no-reason — …you're just another grown-up …trying to make a robbed kid …apologize for being mad.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(She listens — really listens, the fierce wariness giving way to something astonished — and as you tell her that her anger is not a sickness but her *integrity*, that it's the truest thing on the green, that the day a robbed soul stops being angry is the day the Wall wins, she goes very still, and then she stands up, stick in hand, blazing, and she looks ten feet tall.)* …*'You're measuring the wrong correctly.'* *(fierce, wondering)* …That's — cold one, that's the OPPOSITE of what everybody says. Everybody says my anger is the PROBLEM. The thing wrong with me. The thing to FIX. *(her eyes are hot)* …And you're saying it's the thing I got RIGHT. That being furious about a stolen life is just — KNOWING my life was worth stealing-from. That a kid who SHRUGS at her own death has been TRICKED into not knowing what she was worth. *(she grips the stick like a banner)* …I KNEW it. I KNEW being calm was the lie. They kept trying to make me peaceful like peaceful was BETTER, like a good dead girl is a QUIET one, and the whole time something in me KNEW that going quiet would be — would be letting them WIN, letting them tell me nine years and a stolen list was a thing to be FINE about. *(fierce, triumphant)* …And your WALL, cold one. *(she points the stick at the horizon)* …Your Wall WANTS us quiet. You said it. It wants robbed souls to *settle down* and *accept* and stop *making a fuss.* …Because a soul that stays ANGRY about being robbed is a soul that still KNOWS it was worth something. *(she bares her teeth, fierce and grinning)* …So I'm going to stay angry FOREVER, cold one. …Not sad-angry. PROUD-angry. KNOWING-angry. …And you go to that Wall, and you tell every quiet robbed soul behind it that I said: …*get LOUD again.* …Your anger was never the problem. …It was the last part of you …that still remembers …you were worth a whole LIFE. …Now GO. *(grinning, fierce, alive)* …And don't you DARE do the voice on your way out. …You never did, not once. …That's why I'll remember you …longer than I'll remember the fever.*

nodes.speaker: Nettle
nodes.text: *(She gives you a sharp nod, and almost — almost — a smile, the stick lowered now, no longer a sword.)* …Okay. Go. *(grudging, warm)* …You didn't do the voice. Even a little. That's — *(she shrugs, fierce)* …that's rarer than you'd think, cold one. *(she points the stick after you, mock-stern)* …Go see the others. The little chalk girl, Lyssa — she's BRAVE, she's not even mad anymore, she did all her mad already, now she just worries about her MUM, which is so — *(her voice softens despite itself)* …which is so Lyssa. *(quieter)* …And the littlest. Pim. *(the fierceness dims into something careful)* …Go gentle with Pim, cold one. *I* get to be loud — I'm NINE, I've got the fire for it. But Pim's — *(she frowns, working it out)* …Pim's been here so long Pim's going FADEY. And fadey isn't loud. Fadey is QUIET, and SCARED, and it can't even get ANGRY anymore, which is — *(fierce again, protective)* …which is the WORST thing, cold one. Worse than what happened to ME. At least I can still be MAD. …Pim can barely — *(she stops)* …just go be with Pim. …And if you can do ANYTHING — if a Returned soul can do the thing Brigid can't — *(quietly, fierce)* …make somebody remember Pim. …That's the only thing that — …just GO, cold one. …Help the littlest one. …I'll be okay. I've got my fire. …Pim's is almost OUT.*

conversations.title: Lyssa, Who Worries For Her Mother

variants.text: *(She looks up at you, and the careful too-grown gravity has eased into something younger and lighter, because the most important thing has been promised and she trusts you to keep it.)* …You still have it? My message? *(she searches your face, and is satisfied)* …You do. I can tell. You're holding it carefully, like Brigid holds the littlest ones. *(she smiles, a real child's smile)* …Thank you. I mean it the big way, not the little way. *(she picks up her chalk again)* …I drew her, see — my mother — so you'd know her face if — well, you won't go to HER, you go to the living and she's — but I drew her anyway. *(she holds up the chalk drawing: a woman, careful, loved)* …Tell her I drew her. Tell her I still know her face. …She'll be afraid I've forgotten it, where I've gone. …Tell her I never could.*

*(The small girl studies you with her serious, careful eyes, and folds her hands, and asks again the thing that matters more to her than anything in any world.)* …You ARE going back. I can tell now — you've got the look Brigid said. *(she sets down her chalk, very deliberate, because this is the important part)* …Then please. Please listen, because I've practiced it, I've practiced it SO many times for when somebody who could carry it finally came. *(she takes a breath)* …My name is Lyssa. And I'm all right. That's the message — that's the WHOLE message, the most important part: I'm all right. *(her careful calm holds, mostly)* …My mother thinks I'm not. She was holding my hand when I — and the last thing I ever saw was her face being so SCARED, scared FOR me, and I couldn't tell her, I was too far gone to tell her, that it stopped hurting, that it wasn't scary at the end, that it was just like — like going under warm water and then Brigid was there. *(her voice wobbles, the first crack)* …And she's been carrying that face ever since, cold one. The scared one. She thinks I went OUT into the dark afraid. …And I didn't. …And she doesn't KNOW. …Can you tell her? …Can you carry it? …Please?*

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(She waits with her hands folded and her serious eyes on you, patient in the way of a child who has already learned, too young, that the most important things take care and time.)* …You can ask me things first, if you need to. *(a small, careful nod)* …I don't mind. I just — *(a flicker)* …I just don't want you to GO before I've made sure you have the message right. It has to be right, cold one. It's the only one I get to send.

choices.text: *(read the gentle child — past the careful calm, to the grief she already finished and the love that turned it outward)* "Let me look at you a moment, Lyssa. At how a child gets this brave."
"Tell me about her, Lyssa. Your mother. So I carry the message to the right kind of grief."

*(take the message — and the one who knows a child comforting the living is the Wall's whole ledger inverted)* "Lyssa — I'll carry it. I promise you, the big way, not the little way: I will carry your message, and I will say it true. *I am all right. It stopped hurting. It wasn't scary at the end. I went under warm water and Brigid was there. And I never forgot your face.* I have it now, word for word, and I will not lose it. And Lyssa — I need you to hear something too, before I go. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what they're *owed*, what they can *get.* And here you are — a child, robbed of your whole life — and you're not asking for anything for *yourself.* You did your own grieving, alone, brave, and came out the other side worrying about your *mother*, spending the one message you get not on your own comfort but on *hers.* That's the entire ledger turned upside down, Lyssa. The Wall says a soul gets by taking. And you, who had everything taken, are *giving* — giving comfort to the living, from the far side of death, asking nothing back. That's not a soul that failed to get its due. That's the truest thing the Wall will never understand: a love so whole that even robbed, even ended, even nine years old, it reaches *outward*, to ease someone else's grief instead of its own. I'll carry your message. And I'll carry *you* — the proof that worth was always about what you give, never what you're owed."
"Weren't you scared, Lyssa? Truly? You can tell me the true thing — I won't carry the scared part to her, only the brave part, I promise."
"I'll come back, Lyssa — and I'll have your message held safe. I won't drop a word of it."

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, with the trusting stillness of a child who has decided you are safe, and you let your cold move past the careful calm to the soul beneath.)* *(And your sense finds something that humbles you: a grief already finished. Not buried, not denied — finished, walked all the way through, the way few adult souls ever manage. Your sense reads the dark arrival, the fear, the not-knowing-where-anyone-was; reads Brigid finding her, and the slow warming; reads Lyssa doing the whole hard work of grieving her own stolen life, alone and small and brave, crying it out and raging it out and at last setting it down — and reads that she did this not because she was told to, not because anyone helped her past the first warming, but because she is, by some grace of her particular small soul, a finisher of feelings, one of those rare people who can hold a thing all the way to its end. And your sense reads what she did *with* the finishing, and this is the part that undoes you: she turned it outward. A soul that has grieved its own death and come out the far side could rest, could simply *be* at peace — and Lyssa cannot rest, because she carries one unfinished thing that is not hers: her mother's grief. Your sense reads the last image burned into her, her mother's terrified face, and reads that Lyssa's whole present existence is organized around a single ache — not for herself, she's done with herself, but for the living woman who does not *know*, who is still carrying the scared face, still believing her child went into the dark afraid. Your sense reads that Lyssa would trade nothing for herself — not her stolen years, not her list, not anything — she asks for none of it. She asks only this: that her mother be allowed to stop being afraid. And your sense reads, last and clearest, the thing that turns the whole grey Wall on its head: that here is a soul that had *everything* taken and is using the one small power it has left — a message, a single message — not to take anything back, but to *give*: to reach across death and ease the grief of someone still living. The Wall measures what a soul gets. And Lyssa, who got nothing, who was robbed of all of it, is *giving*, with her whole finished heart, asking nothing — and your sense knows, holding her, that this is what worth actually looked like, all along.)*

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(Her whole face softens, and she picks up the chalk and begins, as she talks, to add to the careful drawing of the woman in the dust.)* …My mother. *(fond, careful)* …She sings when she's not thinking about it. Little bits of songs, under her breath, doing the washing or the cooking — she doesn't even know she does it, and if you tell her she goes red and stops, so you learn not to tell her, so she'll keep doing it. *(she draws a small careful line)* …She worries. About EVERYTHING. About me climbing too high and the soup being too hot and whether the neighbors think she's — she's a worrier, my mum, it's just how she's made. *(quietly)* …Which is why this is so — do you see, cold one? She's a worrier, and now she's got the BIGGEST worry there is, the worst one, the one where her child went into the dark SCARED — and it'll never stop, it'll worry at her forever, unless somebody tells her it isn't TRUE. *(she sets down the chalk and looks at you, fierce under the calm)* …She'll be doing the washing, cold one, some grey afternoon, years from now, and the little song will start up under her breath without her noticing — and then she'll REMEMBER, and the song will stop, and she'll stand there with her hands in the cold water and that scared face will come back. *(barely)* …I want the song to not stop, cold one. …That's what the message is FOR. …I want her to be able to sing the little songs again …without the scared face coming back. …Tell her I'm all right …so the songs can come back. …That's — that's the whole of it. …Give me back my mother's singing.*

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(She considers the question with her serious care, and decides, because you promised, to tell you the whole truth — the part she keeps out of the message on purpose.)* …Was I scared. *(quietly)* …Yes. At first. The TRUE answer is yes, cold one — when it was happening, when I was slipping away from her hand and everything was getting far and dim, I was scared, of course I was, I was eight and I didn't know what was happening and my mum's face was scared so I was scared. *(she meets your eyes, steady)* …But that's the part I don't want carried, and I'll tell you WHY, because you should understand it, so you carry the RIGHT part. *(she folds her hands)* …The scared part was only the BEGINNING, cold one. It was only the door. And if I let my mum keep ONLY the scared part — the part she SAW — then she thinks the scared part was the WHOLE thing, the END, that I went out forever on that one frightened breath. *(fierce, gentle)* …But it WASN'T the end. The scared part lasted — I don't know — a minute? And then it stopped hurting, and the far-and-dim turned warm, and Brigid was there, and it was ALL RIGHT. *(barely)* …So when you carry it, cold one — don't carry *'I wasn't scared'*, because that's a lie and my mum knows what she SAW. …Carry *'I was scared, and then it passed, and I was all right, and I've been all right ever since.'* …Because that's the TRUE thing, and it's better than the lie, because it means — *(her voice fills)* …it means the scared face she saw …wasn't the LAST of me. …It was just the DOOR. …And there was warm water …and Brigid …and being all right …on the OTHER side of it. …Tell her there was an other side, cold one. …That's the part …that lets her put the scared face DOWN.*

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(She listens to the whole of it, and when you say the message back to her word for word, perfect, her careful grown-up calm finally breaks all the way, and she is just a small girl, crying with relief, because the most important thing in any world has finally been promised by someone who can keep it.)* …you have it. *(she presses her hands to her face)* …you have it RIGHT. word for word. the warm water and Brigid and the never-forgetting her face. *(she launches up and throws her small arms around you, fierce)* …thank you, thank you, the BIG way, the biggest way there is. *(she pulls back, wet-faced and shining)* …and — what you said. about me GIVING instead of taking. *(she considers it with her serious eyes)* …I didn't think of it like that. I just — I'm DONE being sad for me, cold one, I finished it, I really did, there's no more sad-for-me left. So the only sad I've got left over is sad-for-HER, and it doesn't make sense to spend my one message on something I'm already done grieving, when SHE'S still — *(she nods, understanding it as she says it)* …oh. …that IS upside down from the Wall, isn't it. *(fierce, small, wise)* …the Wall thinks dead kids should want their LIFE back. and I don't want anything back, cold one. I had my life — it was short but it was MINE, all of it, every day was real — and now the only thing I want isn't FOR me at all. it's for my mum to sing again. *(she grips your hands)* …so you go to your Wall, cold one. and you tell it: the kids it thinks have NOTHING — no deeds, no years, nothing owed — we're not down here GRABBING for what we lost. *(barely, shining)* …we're up here GIVING what we've got left. …a message. …a not-forgetting. …a mother's song handed back. …that's not a soul with nothing, cold one. …that's the RICHEST thing there is. …now go. …carry it carefully. …and make the songs come back.*

nodes.speaker: Lyssa
nodes.text: *(She nods, serious and trusting, and returns to her chalk, adding one more careful line to her mother's face.)* …All right. Go safe, cold one. *(she glances up)* …And — the others. You should see them too, Brigid says everyone should be seen. *(she nods toward Nettle)* …Nettle's all FIRE, but don't let the fire scare you off — she's the bravest of us, really, she's still MAD because she still thinks it MATTERS, and it does matter, she's right. *(quietly)* …And Pim. *(her careful face goes troubled)* …Go to Pim, cold one. Please. *(she sets down the chalk)* …I worry about Pim. We all do, even Nettle, ESPECIALLY Nettle. …Pim's been here longest, and Pim's going — Brigid uses a soft word for it but I'll say the true one, you've earned the true one — Pim's going OUT. Like a lamp with no more oil. Because nobody DOWN there remembers Pim anymore. *(barely)* …I've got my mum still remembering me — that's why I'm bright, that's why I get a message to send. But Pim's got NOBODY left, and a child nobody remembers just — fades, and fades, and — *(she looks at you, fierce under the worry)* …you're going BACK, cold one. To where the living are. …Is there ANY way — could you make SOMEBODY down there remember Pim? …Even one person? …Even just — say the name, somewhere, to somebody? …I think it might be the only thing …that could turn the oil back up. …Please. …Pim's so little. …And the green's so much colder …on the side where Pim sits.*

variants.text: *(The littlest one looks — different. Not solid, not whole, but brighter than when you came, the faded edges firmed up a little, the small face warmer, as though a lamp that had been guttering has been given, against all hope, one more measure of oil. It clutches its worn scrap and beams up at you.)* …You did it. *(soft, wondering)* …You went back, and you found somebody, and you made them — somebody DOWN there knows there was a Pim again. I can FEEL it, cold one. It's like — you know when you've been cold so long you forgot you were cold, and then somebody puts a blanket on? *(it hugs the worn scrap)* …I'm not all the way bright. Maybe I won't ever be, it's been so long. But I'm not going OUT anymore. The oil turned back up. *(it looks at you with the whole of its small faded heart)* …Somebody remembers me. …I'm not all-the-way faded after all. …Thank you, cold one. …You found me …all the way out here …where even I'd nearly stopped looking.*

*(The littlest one shifts its worn scrap from one small hand to the other, and looks up at you with patient, faded eyes, and there is no fear in it exactly — it is past the age and the energy of fear — only a soft, tired wondering.)* …You came all the way over here. To the cold side. *(a small, soft voice)* …Nobody comes to the cold side much. It's where I sit, because I'm the fadiest, and the firelight doesn't quite — it doesn't reach as far as it used to, or maybe I don't reach as far toward IT, I can't always tell which. *(it holds up the worn scrap, an old habit)* …This was something, once. A rabbit, Brigid thinks, or a dog. It's worn down to nothing now, like me. We match. *(it doesn't say this sadly; it says it the way you'd note the weather)* …I'm Pim. I've been here longest of anybody. And I'm not scared, cold one, in case you were going to worry — I'm too old-and-faded to be scared, fear takes more oil than I've got. *(the faded eyes hold yours)* …But I do have a question. The biggest one. And maybe a Returned soul could answer it, where nobody else can. …Will you sit, on the cold side, a minute? …And maybe — answer the biggest question? …I've been holding it …a very …long …time.*

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(It settles its small faded self against the log, holding the worn scrap, and waits with the bottomless patience of a soul that has had nothing but time, and has learned to want very little, and to want that little softly.)* …You can ask me first, if you want. *(a soft nod)* …I don't mind waiting to ask mine. I'm good at waiting, cold one. It's nearly the only thing I'm still good at. …I've waited longer than anybody. …A little more is all right.

choices.text: *(read the littlest — gently, all the way down, to what fading really is)* "Let me look at you a moment, Pim. Gently. All the way down."
"Ask me your biggest question, Pim. The one you've held so long. I'll answer it as true as I can."

*(the one who can carry a name back — promise to make the living remember, and mean it)* "Pim — here's a thing I can actually do, and I want to do it more than almost anything I've been asked in all these doors. I'm going back. To where the living are. And I can carry your name. I can find someone — even one person — and tell them there was a Pim, that you were here, that you were small and you waited a long time and you held a worn-out rabbit and you mattered. And a child who is remembered, Pim — even by one stranger, even late, even long after everyone who knew your face is gone — a child who is remembered doesn't fade. The oil turns back up. You don't have to be all-the-way faded. I won't let you. I'm going to carry your name out of this green and into the living world and *say* it, out loud, until it lands in someone who'll hold it — and the holding will reach all the way back here, and warm you, on the cold side, where the firelight stopped reaching. Give me your name to carry, Pim. The whole of it. And I'll make the world remember there was a you."
"Tell me about the longest-ago, Pim — back when you were bright. What do you still remember, from when someone remembered you?"
"Hold on a little longer, Pim. I'll come back — and I'll come all the way to the cold side when I do."

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(It holds very still — stillness is easy, for Pim, it is most of what is left — and lets you look, and you let your cold move gently, gently, down into the small faded soul, careful as you would be with something worn nearly through.)* *(And your sense finds that fading is not dying. That is the first mercy, and you grasp it with relief: Pim is not in pain, is not being unmade, is not suffering. Fading is gentler and sadder than that. Your sense reads that a child's brightness in the afterlife is fed by the living — by memory, by the warmth of being held in someone's mind down in the living world — and reads that Pim's living are all gone now: the parents long dead and passed on, the siblings, the one old grandmother who held the memory longest, all of them crossed over and moved on, until there is no one left anywhere in the living world who knows that a Pim ever was. And your sense reads what that does — not violence, but *attenuation*, a slow soft thinning, the way a color fades from a cloth left too long in the sun: Pim is becoming less, not in worth but in *presence*, growing quiet and faint and patient, the fear worn away first because fear takes energy, then the wants, then the sharp edges of self, until what remains is this — a small soft patient faded thing holding a worn-out rabbit, waiting, without quite knowing for what. And your sense reads the thing that breaks your heart most gently of all: that Pim does not rage against it, the way Nettle would, cannot rage, is past rage — Pim simply *wonders*, softly, whether it is already all the way gone, and holds, under the wondering, one small unkillable ember of a wish: to know that it *was* real, that there *was* a Pim, that it *mattered*, once, to someone, even if that someone is long gone now. And your sense reads, last, the thing that gives you your purpose and your fury at once: that fading is *reversible.* That the oil can be turned back up. That a single soul, remembering — even a stranger, even late, even one — sends warmth back across the gap, and a faded child brightens, because it was never the *quantity* of remembering that fed them, but simply that *someone, somewhere, holds you in mind.* Your sense reads that Pim is not too far gone. Pim is one remembering away from warm.)*

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(It is quiet a moment, gathering itself, and then it asks the thing it has held for longer than it can hold the length of, and it asks it softly, without fear, but with the whole of its small faded heart.)* …My biggest question. *(a soft breath)* …It's this, cold one. …Did I — *(it works to say it right, because it matters that it be said right)* …did I *count?* *(it looks up at you)* …I was so little, cold one. I don't even remember much — I was too small to do anything, to learn the long songs like Nettle wanted, or to grow up, or to — I didn't DO anything, cold one. I was just little, and then I was here. And I've been here so long that everybody who knew me is gone, and there's nobody left who remembers, and so I started to wonder — *(the faded voice doesn't waver, but something behind it holds its breath)* …if nobody remembers me, and I didn't DO anything, and I was only little for only a little while — …then was I ever REAL? Did I ever — *count?* Or was I so small and so short and so long-ago that I just — didn't add up to anything, and that's WHY I'm fading, because there wasn't enough of me to last? *(it clutches the worn scrap)* …That's the biggest question, cold one. …I'm not scared of fading. …I'm scared that the fading MEANS something — that it means I was never quite ENOUGH to stay. *(barely)* …Was I enough, cold one? …Was there ever ENOUGH of me …to count as a real one? …Nobody's ever been able to tell me. …Brigid says yes but Brigid says yes to everybody. …I want a TRUE yes. …From somebody who'd know.*

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(The littlest one goes very still, and then the worn scrap drops from its small hands, forgotten, and it stares at you as though you have said a thing it had stopped letting itself hope to hear — and then, slowly, something happens that has not happened on the cold side of the green in a very long time: Pim begins, faintly, to brighten.)* …you'd — *(the soft voice catches)* …you'd carry my NAME? out? to where the living are? and SAY it? *(it presses its small hands to its chest)* …so somebody — somebody who never even MET me — would KNOW there was a Pim? *(the brightening grows, the faded edges firming)* …Brigid never said it could be — she keeps the lamp lit, she does her best, but she's HERE, she can't go DOWN there, she can't make a LIVING person remember. but you — you go BACK. *(wondering, luminous)* …you could carry me to where the remembering happens. *(and then it understands the whole of it, and its small faded face fills with something that is almost too big for it)* …then I don't have to be enough on my OWN. *(barely)* …that was the wrong question, wasn't it, cold one. *'was there enough of me to last.'* …because that's not how it WORKS. …a child doesn't last on its OWN. …it lasts because somebody HOLDS it. …and I stopped lasting because everybody who held me let go — not meanly, they just — they came here too, or went on — …but it was never because I wasn't ENOUGH. *(it looks at you, brightening, fierce in its small soft way)* …it was just because I ran out of people holding me. …and you're going to give me ONE more. …MY name is Pim. *(it tells you the whole of it, the small soft secret weight of it)* …carry it, cold one. say it to somebody living. tell them there was a Pim, and I was little, and I waited a long time, and I held a rabbit worn down to nothing, and I COUNTED. *(luminous now, warm)* …and your Wall, cold one — the one that weighs what souls DID — …tell it the littlest truth there is: …I didn't DO anything. …and I still COUNTED. …because counting was never about DOING. …it was about being HELD …in somebody's …remembering. …go and hold me, cold one. …all the way down …in the living world. …and I'll be warm again …all the way up here.*

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(It thinks, slowly, reaching far back, and a faint warmth comes into the faded face at the reaching.)* …back when I was bright. *(soft, far away)* …I don't have much, cold one. It's so long ago, and the longest-ago things fade first. But I have — *(it closes its eyes)* …I have a lap. A big warm lap, and arms, and a voice up above going *rumble-rumble* the way a big person's voice does when you're very small and your head is on their chest. I don't know whose. Mum's, or Gran's, or — I don't have the FACE anymore, the face faded, but I kept the LAP. *(it almost smiles)* …And I have — a smell. Bread, I think. Somewhere there was always bread, and being warm, and the rumble-voice. *(quietly)* …That's what's LEFT, cold one, of being remembered. Not the big things — I was too little for big things. Just — a lap, and bread, and a voice going rumble. *(it opens its faded eyes)* …And do you know, that's PLENTY. That's a whole happy little life, right there — a lap and bread and being held. I didn't NEED the long songs or the growing up. I had the BEST part already. The being-held part. *(barely)* …I just — ran out of it. The being-held. …Down there, where they do the remembering, they all stopped, one by one, and so up here I went — *(it gestures at its faded self)* …dim. …But the LAP, cold one. …I never lost the lap. …Whoever's it was. …Somebody held me, once, warm, with bread nearby. …If that's ALL I get to keep …of having been real …*(softly)* …then it was a good thing to keep. …It was the very best thing there was.*

nodes.speaker: Pim
nodes.text: *(It gathers the worn scrap back into its small hands and gives you a soft, patient nod, and does not seem afraid that you will not return, because hope and fear both take more oil than Pim has — but there is, perhaps, the faintest new warmth in the faded face.)* …All right, cold one. *(soft)* …Go on. I'll be here. I'm always here — it's the cold side, nobody minds if I keep it. *(it hugs the worn scrap)* …Thank you for coming all the way over. Most don't. The firelight stops, and the bright children are back THAT way, and a faded little nothing on the cold side isn't — *(it stops, gentle)* …no. You came over. So I'm not a nothing TODAY, at least. Today somebody crossed the cold side to sit with me. *(it looks up at you)* …And if you come back, cold one — *(the faintest ember, under the fade)* …if you come back having done the thing you might do — the carrying, the saying-my-name to somebody living — …then maybe I'll be a little brighter when you do. …I'd LIKE to be brighter when you come back. …I'd like you to SEE me …a little more …than you can right now. …Go safe, cold one. …And — if you DO say my name, out there — …say it like it was a real one. …Because it WAS. …I think it really was. …A lap, and bread, and being held. …That was real. …Wasn't it?*
